subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: Causation: The But-For Test, Material Contribution, and Novus Actus Interveniens

questions.question: What is the primary test for establishing factual causation in Irish negligence law?

options.A: The but-for test — the plaintiff must prove that but for the defendant's breach of duty, the damage would not have occurred
options.B: The material contribution test — the plaintiff must show the defendant's breach materially contributed to the harm
options.C: The balance of risks test — the plaintiff must show the defendant's breach more than doubled the risk of harm
options.D: The Glencar foreseeability test — the plaintiff must establish that the harm was reasonably foreseeable at the time of the breach

questions.explanation: The but-for test is the primary test for factual causation. The plaintiff must prove, on the balance of probabilities, that but for the defendant's breach of duty, the damage would not have occurred. If the plaintiff would have suffered the same damage regardless of the breach, the but-for test is not satisfied and no causal link is established. Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital [1969] is the leading illustration.

questions.question: In Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969], the court found no liability despite established negligence. Why?

options.A: The hospital held a valid statutory exemption from liability for emergency treatment provided outside normal hours
options.B: Even if the hospital had treated the patient promptly, he would have died anyway — the but-for test was not satisfied
options.C: The patient's consumption of arsenic was treated as a novus actus interveniens breaking the chain of causation
options.D: The plaintiff failed to adduce expert medical evidence establishing what the outcome of timely treatment would have been

questions.explanation: In Barnett [1969] 1 QB 428, although the hospital was negligent in failing to examine and treat the patient, the but-for test was not satisfied. Medical evidence established that even if the patient had been treated promptly, he would have died — the arsenic had already been absorbed to a lethal level and there was no treatment that could have saved him. Negligence without causation = no liability.

questions.question: What fundamental principle does Barnett v Chelsea Hospital [1969] establish about the relationship between negligence and causation?

options.A: A defendant who is negligent is automatically liable for all harm that follows from their act
options.B: Causation is presumed once negligence has been established, unless the defendant rebuts the presumption
options.C: Negligence and causation are separate questions — a defendant can be negligent and yet face no liability if the breach did not actually cause the harm
options.D: The standard of proof for causation is higher than the balance of probabilities because it requires scientific certainty

questions.explanation: Barnett v Chelsea Hospital establishes the fundamental principle that negligence and causation are distinct elements of a negligence claim. A defendant may be in breach of their duty of care and yet escape all liability if the plaintiff cannot establish that the breach actually caused the damage. Establishing a breach does not trigger automatic liability.

questions.question: In Baker v Willoughby [1970], how did the House of Lords address the problem of multiple successive torts?

options.A: It applied the material contribution doctrine and apportioned liability proportionately between the two tortfeasors
options.B: It held that the supervening robbery was a novus actus interveniens that extinguished the original defendant's liability
options.C: It applied the but-for test strictly and found neither tortfeasor liable because neither alone caused the final outcome
options.D: It held the original tortfeasor liable for the full loss of the leg, refusing to allow a subsequent independent tort to extinguish the pre-existing liability

questions.explanation: Baker v Willoughby [1970] AC 467 dealt with the problem of supervening torts. The original defendant had injured the plaintiff's leg. A subsequent robbery (an independent tort) resulted in amputation of that leg. The House of Lords held the original tortfeasor remained liable for the full pre-existing loss — the supervening tort could not extinguish the original liability.

questions.question: In the Irish case of Philip v Ryan [2004], what did the Irish High Court hold regarding loss of a chance?

options.A: Loss of a real and substantial chance is actionable in Ireland, even where it falls below 50%
options.B: Loss of a chance is never actionable in Ireland — the plaintiff must always prove causation to the full balance of probabilities
options.C: Loss of a chance is actionable only where the defendant's negligence was intentional rather than careless
options.D: Irish law follows the English Gregg v Scott approach, requiring proof that the better outcome had a probability exceeding 50%

questions.explanation: Philip v Ryan [2004] IEHC is the key Irish authority on loss of a chance. The Irish High Court recognised that loss of a real and substantial chance — even one below the 50% threshold — can be actionable in Ireland. This is more plaintiff-friendly than the English position in Gregg v Scott [2005], where the House of Lords generally required the plaintiff to prove a greater than 50% probability of a better outcome.

questions.question: In Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] HoL, why was the employer held liable despite the impossibility of proving which source of dust caused the plaintiff's pneumoconiosis?

options.A: The employer was strictly liable under health and safety legislation for all industrial diseases contracted by employees on the premises
options.B: The guilty dust — from equipment the employer was in breach of duty to maintain — had made a material contribution, more than minimal, to the development of the disease
options.C: The court applied the but-for test and found, on balance, that the guilty dust had more probably than not caused the disease
options.D: The employer was held jointly and severally liable with the innocent dust sources as concurrent wrongdoers under the Civil Liability Act 1961

questions.explanation: Bonnington Castings Ltd v Wardlaw [1956] AC 613 is the foundational authority for the material contribution doctrine. The silica dust came from two sources — 'guilty' dust (from negligently maintained equipment) and 'innocent' dust (not attributable to any breach). Since it was scientifically impossible to isolate which source caused the disease, the House of Lords held the employer liable because the guilty dust had made a material contribution — more than minimal — to the development of the disease.

questions.question: What additional step did the House of Lords take in McGhee v National Coal Board [1973], beyond the Bonnington material contribution doctrine?

options.A: It held that mere exposure to a harmful substance is always sufficient to establish causation without any further proof
options.B: It applied the material contribution doctrine only where the employer had a specific statutory duty to provide the missing precaution
options.C: It held that by materially increasing the risk of harm, the employer was treated as having materially contributed to the harm itself
options.D: It applied the Fairchild exception and held each employer jointly liable for the entire disease under the Fairchild principle

questions.explanation: McGhee v National Coal Board [1973] AC 729 extended Bonnington by equating material increase in risk with material contribution to harm. Mr McGhee's dermatitis was caused by brick dust, with innocent exposure during work and guilty exposure on the way home (due to the employer's failure to provide washing facilities). The House of Lords held that materially increasing the risk of a disease is treated as equivalent to making a material contribution to the disease itself.

questions.question: In Wilsher v Essex Area Health Authority [1988] HoL, why did the House of Lords refuse to apply the material contribution doctrine?

options.A: The plaintiff was a premature baby and the material contribution doctrine cannot apply to claims by minors
options.B: The defendant had acted within accepted clinical practice and was therefore not negligent
options.C: The plaintiff's condition was entirely pre-existing and unrelated to any act of the defendant
options.D: There were five entirely distinct potential causes of the blindness condition, only one attributable to the defendant's negligence — this was a multiple-cause scenario, not a single-agent scenario

questions.explanation: Wilsher v Essex AHA [1988] AC 1074 is critical in defining the limits of the material contribution doctrine. A premature baby suffered a blindness condition. Five distinct medical conditions could each independently have caused it, only one being attributable to the defendant's negligence (excess oxygen). The House of Lords refused to apply Bonnington/McGhee because those cases involved one type of agent from multiple sources; Wilsher involved multiple entirely different potential causes. The plaintiff had to satisfy the but-for test — and could not.

questions.question: What is the key distinction between the Bonnington/McGhee scenarios and the Wilsher scenario, which is frequently tested on the FE-1 paper?

options.A: Bonnington and McGhee involved one type of harmful agent from multiple sources — material contribution applies; Wilsher involved multiple entirely distinct potential causes — the but-for test must be used
options.B: Bonnington and McGhee involved intentional wrongdoing by employers; Wilsher involved purely careless medical treatment
options.C: Bonnington and McGhee were statutory liability cases; Wilsher was a common law negligence case
options.D: Bonnington and McGhee applied strict liability; Wilsher applied the ordinary negligence standard with the but-for test

questions.explanation: The Bonnington-Wilsher distinction is one of the most tested areas in the FE-1 paper. The material contribution doctrine applies where a single type of causative agent came from multiple sources — one negligent, one not — and it is impossible to identify which source caused the disease. Where, as in Wilsher, there are multiple entirely distinct potential causes (each independently capable of causing the condition), the plaintiff must prove their case under the but-for test.

questions.question: The Fairchild exception established in Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services [2002] HoL applies primarily to which type of case?

options.A: All industrial disease cases involving exposure to any type of harmful dust or particulate substance
options.B: Mesothelioma cases where multiple employers negligently exposed the worker to asbestos and science cannot identify which specific fibre caused the disease
options.C: All product liability cases involving defective goods manufactured by multiple separate defendants
options.D: All clinical negligence cases where multiple healthcare providers treated the same patient

questions.explanation: The Fairchild exception [2002] UKHL 22 was developed specifically for mesothelioma — a disease caused only by asbestos fibres, in a way that science cannot trace to any specific exposure. Where multiple employers each negligently exposed a worker to asbestos, and it is impossible to identify which employer's fibres caused the mesothelioma, the Fairchild exception allows each employer who materially increased the risk to be held liable. The exception is currently primarily confined to mesothelioma.

questions.question: In Fairchild v Glenhaven Funeral Services [2002], on what basis were multiple employers held liable?

options.A: Each employer was held individually liable under the but-for test applied separately to each employment period
options.B: The court applied market share liability, apportioning damages according to each employer's relative share of asbestos exposure
options.C: Each employer who materially increased the risk of mesothelioma was held liable, even though science could not identify which employer's fibre actually caused the disease
options.D: The employers were treated as a single enterprise and held jointly and severally liable without any apportionment between them

questions.explanation: In Fairchild [2002] UKHL 22, the House of Lords developed a special causation rule for mesothelioma: where a single disease-causing agent (asbestos) can only cause mesothelioma in one way, and multiple employers each negligently exposed the worker to that agent, each employer who materially increased the risk was held liable. The Fairchild exception operates as a departure from the strict but-for test in circumstances of unavoidable scientific uncertainty.

questions.question: In Breslin v Corcoran [2003] IESC, the defendant left his keys in his car. A thief stole it and injured the plaintiff. What did the Irish Supreme Court hold?

options.A: The defendant was liable because leaving keys in a car creates a foreseeable risk of theft and subsequent injury
options.B: The defendant was liable as a concurrent wrongdoer alongside the thief under the Civil Liability Act 1961
options.C: The defendant was partially liable because leaving keys in the car materially contributed to the conditions that enabled the theft
options.D: The voluntary, independent act of the thief broke the chain of causation between the defendant's carelessness and the plaintiff's injury

questions.explanation: Breslin v Corcoran [2003] 2 IR 203 is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on novus actus interveniens by a third party. The court held that the voluntary, independent criminal act of the thief constituted a novus actus interveniens that broke the chain of causation. The defendant's negligence in leaving keys in the car was not legally regarded as the cause of the plaintiff's injury — the thief's independent decision to drive the stolen vehicle was.

questions.question: Under Irish law, when does a third party's intervening act NOT break the chain of causation?

options.A: Where the third party's act was foreseeable, or was the very risk against which the defendant owed a duty to guard
options.B: Where the third party acted negligently rather than intentionally or deliberately
options.C: Where the third party was acting in a professional capacity at the time of the intervening act
options.D: Where the defendant had already taken some precautions against the risk, even if those precautions proved insufficient

questions.explanation: A third party's intervening act will NOT break the chain of causation where it was foreseeable to the defendant at the time of the original breach, or where it was the very risk against which the defendant was under a duty to guard. In such cases, the intervening act is treated as a realisation of the foreseeable risk rather than as an independent supervening cause. See Reeves v Commissioner of Police [2000].

questions.question: In Reeves v Commissioner of Police for the Metropolis [2000] HoL, a prisoner with known suicidal tendencies took their own life while in police custody. Did the suicide break the chain of causation?

options.A: Yes — suicide is always a voluntary act in law and therefore automatically constitutes a novus actus interveniens
options.B: No — the very purpose of the duty of care owed by the police was to guard against this exact risk, so the act did not break the chain
options.C: Yes — the prisoner was mentally capable of making rational decisions and freely chose to act
options.D: No — the Corr v IBC principle applied because the prisoner had a pre-existing diagnosed psychiatric condition

questions.explanation: Reeves v Commissioner of Police [2000] 1 AC 360 illustrates that where the very purpose of a duty is to prevent a particular harm (in this case, suicide by a person known to be a suicide risk), the occurrence of that harm cannot break the chain of causation. The police owed a specific duty to prevent self-harm by this individual; the suicide was the realisation of that specific risk, not an independent supervening cause.

questions.question: In McKew v Holland and Hannen and Cubitts [1969] HoL, the plaintiff was originally injured by the defendant and later fell on a steep staircase, causing further injury. What did the House of Lords hold?

options.A: The defendant was liable for both injuries as they were all causally connected to the original negligent act
options.B: The plaintiff's fall was an unavoidable consequence of the original injury and did not break the chain of causation
options.C: The plaintiff's own unreasonable decision to descend a steep staircase without support, knowing his leg was weak, constituted a novus actus breaking the chain
options.D: Both the defendant and the plaintiff were concurrent wrongdoers and liability was apportioned equally between them

questions.explanation: McKew v Holland and Hannen [1969] 3 All ER 1621 established that an unreasonable, voluntary act by the plaintiff can constitute a novus actus interveniens breaking the chain. The plaintiff knew his leg was weak after the original injury and chose to descend a steep staircase without a handrail or support. This unreasonable choice was treated as a new, independent cause of the subsequent injury.

questions.question: In Corr v IBC Vehicles Ltd [2008] HoL, a workplace head injury led to severe clinical depression and ultimately suicide. Did the suicide break the chain of causation?

options.A: Yes — suicide is per se a voluntary act that always breaks the chain of causation, regardless of the victim's mental state
options.B: Yes — the depression had developed over a long period, severing the causal link with the original workplace injury
options.C: No — the employer owed a duty to guard against suicide as a foreseeable result of the workplace injury
options.D: No — the severe clinical depression caused by the head injury had destroyed Mr Corr's capacity for free and rational choice, so the suicide was not a truly voluntary act

questions.explanation: Corr v IBC Vehicles [2008] UKHL 13 distinguishes McKew by focusing on the plaintiff's capacity for free and rational choice. Mr Corr's severe clinical depression — itself a foreseeable consequence of the head injury — had so impaired his mental capacity that his suicide was not a free and voluntary act. Therefore, the suicide was part of the foreseeable chain of harm flowing from the defendant's negligence and did not break that chain.

questions.question: What is the key distinction between McKew v Holland and Hannen and Cubitts [1969] and Corr v IBC Vehicles Ltd [2008] on the novus actus doctrine?

options.A: In McKew, the plaintiff made a free, unreasonable choice; in Corr, the plaintiff's condition had destroyed his capacity for free and rational choice
options.B: In McKew, the defendant was a public authority; in Corr, the defendant was a private employer
options.C: McKew involved physical injury only; Corr involved psychiatric injury to which different causation rules apply
options.D: In McKew, the intervening act was negligent; in Corr, the intervening act was intentional and deliberate

questions.explanation: The McKew-Corr distinction is fundamental. In McKew, the plaintiff made a free, unreasonable decision knowing the risks — this constituted a novus actus. In Corr, the severe clinical depression (itself a foreseeable consequence of the defendant's negligence) had destroyed the plaintiff's capacity for free and rational decision-making. Without the capacity for a free choice, the act could not constitute a novus actus.

questions.question: As a general rule, does negligent medical treatment of an injury caused by the defendant break the chain of causation?

options.A: Yes — negligent medical treatment always constitutes a novus actus interveniens as it is an independent professional act
options.B: No — negligent medical treatment of an injury caused by the defendant does not generally break the chain, as it is a foreseeable consequence of causing the original injury
options.C: Yes if the medical negligence was gross; no if it was merely ordinary carelessness
options.D: It depends on whether the medical negligence was committed by a specialist consultant or a general practitioner

questions.explanation: The general rule is that negligent medical treatment of an injury caused by the defendant does not break the chain of causation. It is foreseeable that a person who is injured will seek medical treatment, and it is also foreseeable that such treatment may itself be negligently provided. The original tortfeasor therefore remains liable for harm caused by the negligent treatment of the original injury.

questions.question: In Webb v Barclays Bank [2001], negligent medical advice led to an unnecessary amputation. Did this break the chain of causation?

options.A: Yes — the amputation was far more serious than the original injury and was therefore too remote from the defendant's negligence
options.B: Yes — the medical professionals made an independent judgment, which constituted a free and voluntary act breaking the chain
options.C: No — the negligent medical treatment was a foreseeable consequence of the original injury caused by the defendant
options.D: No — but the defendant was only liable for the amputation itself, not for the original underlying condition

questions.explanation: Webb v Barclays Bank [2001] EWCA Civ 1141 applied the general rule that negligent medical treatment does not break the chain of causation. The negligent medical advice leading to unnecessary amputation was a foreseeable consequence of the original injury for which the defendant was responsible. The defendant remained liable for the full consequences, including those arising from the subsequent medical negligence.

questions.question: The egg-shell skull rule in Irish tort law means that:

options.A: A defendant is only liable for harm that would have been suffered by a person of average physical health and constitution
options.B: A defendant may rely on the plaintiff's pre-existing condition to reduce the quantum of damages proportionately
options.C: A defendant is not liable for harm that is aggravated by the plaintiff's unforeseeable genetic predisposition to disease
options.D: A defendant must take their victim as they find them — if the victim has a pre-existing vulnerability, the defendant is liable for the full extent of the harm, provided the type of initial injury was foreseeable

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule (also called the thin skull rule) requires a defendant to take the plaintiff as they find them. Where the plaintiff has a pre-existing physical or psychological vulnerability that makes the consequences of the defendant's tort worse than would otherwise be expected, the defendant is nonetheless liable for the full extent of those consequences — provided that the type of initial injury was foreseeable. The rule is illustrated by Smith v Leech Brain [1962].

questions.question: In Smith v Leech Brain and Co Ltd [1962], how was the egg-shell skull rule applied?

options.A: A worker suffered a burn from the defendant's negligence; the burn triggered a pre-existing cancerous condition and the worker died — the defendant was liable for the full harm because a burn was a foreseeable type of injury
options.B: The defendant was not liable for the cancer because it was an entirely independent disease unconnected to the burn
options.C: Liability was limited to the burn itself — the subsequent cancer exceeded what was reasonably foreseeable and was therefore too remote
options.D: The court applied the Wagon Mound foreseeability test and found the cancer was too remote to attract liability

questions.explanation: Smith v Leech Brain and Co Ltd [1962] 2 QB 405 applied the egg-shell skull rule. The defendant's negligence caused a worker to be splashed with molten metal. This burn triggered a pre-existing cancerous condition that ultimately killed him. The court held the defendant fully liable: the type of initial injury (a burn) was foreseeable; the defendant must take the victim as found — including the pre-existing vulnerability that made the consequences so severe.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the burden and standard of proof for establishing causation in Irish negligence?

options.A: The defendant bears the burden of disproving causation once the plaintiff establishes a breach of duty
options.B: The plaintiff must prove causation on the balance of probabilities — more probable than not that the breach caused the harm
options.C: In complex medical cases, causation must be established to a higher standard than the balance of probabilities
options.D: Causation is automatically presumed once negligence is established, unless the defendant proves the absence of causation

questions.explanation: The plaintiff bears the burden of proving causation on the balance of probabilities — meaning it is more probable than not (i.e., more than 50% likely) that the defendant's breach caused the damage. This standard applies in all negligence cases, including complex medical cases. There is no higher standard for causation than balance of probabilities.

questions.question: The material contribution doctrine applies in which scenario?

options.A: Where there are multiple defendants each of whom contributed to a distinct and separate head of damage
options.B: Where the defendant's breach was a substantial cause of the harm, exceeding the but-for threshold but insufficient alone
options.C: Where one type of harmful agent from multiple sources — one negligent, one not — combined to cause the harm, and it is scientifically impossible to isolate which source caused it
options.D: Where the plaintiff suffered cumulative harm from a series of acts by the same defendant over a long period of time

questions.explanation: The material contribution doctrine, as established in Bonnington Castings [1956], applies where: (1) a single type of harmful agent came from multiple sources; (2) at least one source was negligent (guilty) and at least one was not (innocent); and (3) it is scientifically impossible to determine which source's contribution caused the disease. In those circumstances, proof that the guilty source made a more than minimal (material) contribution to the harm is sufficient for liability.

questions.question: In Davey v Sligo County Council [2023], the Irish Court of Appeal confirmed what principle regarding intervening acts?

options.A: Any act by a third party, whether or not foreseeable, automatically breaks the chain of causation in Irish law
options.B: Local authorities can never be held liable for intervening acts of third parties on public roads
options.C: An intervening act by a third party breaks the chain of causation wherever the third party was an adult of full legal capacity
options.D: An intervening act does not break the chain of causation where the defendant's original failure materially contributed to the conditions in which the intervening act occurred

questions.explanation: Davey v Sligo County Council [2023] IECA is a recent Irish Court of Appeal authority confirming that an intervening act will not break the chain of causation where the original defendant's failure materially contributed to the conditions that enabled the intervening act to occur. This reflects the broader principle that foreseeability and contribution to conditions are key to the novus actus analysis.

questions.question: The but-for test presents difficulties with multiple sufficient causes. In Baker v Willoughby [1970], what was the problem with strict application of the but-for test?

options.A: A strict application would have allowed the original tortfeasor to escape all liability after a supervening tort (amputation by the robbers) extinguished the pre-existing injury — which would be unjust
options.B: Both events occurred simultaneously, making it impossible to establish which came first for the purposes of the but-for analysis
options.C: Strict application would have held the victim to be the sole legal cause of his loss because he had placed himself in a dangerous situation
options.D: Neither defendant could be identified with certainty as the cause of the final state of the plaintiff's injury

questions.explanation: The difficulty in Baker v Willoughby was that a strict application of the but-for test would have allowed the original tortfeasor (who injured the leg) to escape liability after a subsequent independent tort (a robbery causing amputation) effectively 'replaced' the original injury. The House of Lords refused to allow this absurd result — the original tortfeasor's liability had already crystallised and the supervening event could not extinguish it.

questions.question: What is a 'novus actus interveniens' in the context of causation in Irish tort law?

options.A: A new duty of care that arises after the initial negligent act and is owed to the plaintiff by the original defendant
options.B: A new intervening act — by a third party or by the plaintiff — that is sufficiently voluntary, independent, and unforeseeable to break the chain of causation between the defendant's breach and the plaintiff's loss
options.C: A new legal proceeding commenced by the plaintiff against a third party that extinguishes their existing claim against the original defendant
options.D: A subsequent increase in the severity of the plaintiff's injury attributed to the plaintiff's failure to seek timely medical treatment

questions.explanation: Novus actus interveniens (Latin: 'new intervening act') refers to an event — whether the voluntary act of a third party, the voluntary act of the plaintiff, or (in rare cases) an Act of God — that is so independent and unforeseeable that it breaks the legal chain of causation between the defendant's breach of duty and the plaintiff's loss. Once the chain is broken, the defendant escapes liability for the subsequent harm.

questions.question: In the context of the material contribution doctrine, what does 'material' contribution mean?

options.A: The defendant's contribution must have been equal to or greater than all other contributing sources combined
options.B: The defendant's contribution must have been the principal or predominant cause of the harm suffered
options.C: The defendant's contribution must have been more than minimal — a de minimis contribution is insufficient to establish liability
options.D: The defendant's contribution must be capable of precise scientific quantification as a percentage of the total harmful exposure

questions.explanation: In Bonnington Castings [1956], the House of Lords held that the guilty dust must have made a 'material' contribution to the disease — meaning more than minimal or de minimis. The defendant does not need to have been the dominant or primary source of the harm. Any contribution that goes beyond trivial is sufficient to establish liability under the material contribution doctrine.

questions.question: Why does the egg-shell skull rule impose full liability even for unforeseeable aggravation of the plaintiff's harm?

options.A: Because the law requires defendants to compensate for all consequences of their negligence, regardless of probability or foreseeability
options.B: Because the aggravated harm is treated as a separate and distinct tort for which strict liability automatically applies
options.C: Because foreseeability of the specific aggravation is wholly irrelevant once a duty of care and a breach are established
options.D: Because once the type of initial injury was foreseeable, the defendant must take the victim as found — including pre-existing conditions that amplify the consequences of that injury beyond what would ordinarily be expected

questions.explanation: The egg-shell skull rule operates as a qualification of the remoteness principle: once the type of injury was foreseeable, the defendant is responsible for the full extent of the harm, even if that extent was unforeseeable because of the victim's pre-existing vulnerability. The rule is grounded in the principle that defendants must take plaintiffs as they find them. See Smith v Leech Brain [1962].

questions.question: In Philip v Ryan [2004], how does the Irish approach to loss of a chance differ from the English position in Gregg v Scott [2005]?

options.A: Irish law (Philip v Ryan) allows recovery for the loss of a real and substantial chance even below 50%; English law (Gregg v Scott) generally requires proof that the better outcome had a greater than 50% probability
options.B: Irish law requires proof of a better-outcome probability exceeding 50%; English law allows recovery for any material chance, however small
options.C: Irish law and English law are now identical — both require proof on the balance of probabilities for loss of a chance claims
options.D: Irish law requires scientific certainty before loss of a chance claims may proceed; English law uses the balance of probabilities

questions.explanation: Philip v Ryan [2004] IEHC and Gregg v Scott [2005] UKHL 2 reflect different approaches. In Ireland, the High Court recognised that loss of a real and substantial chance can be actionable even where the probability falls below 50%. In England, the House of Lords in Gregg v Scott generally required the plaintiff to establish that the better outcome would have occurred — requiring a probability exceeding 50%. Irish law is more plaintiff-friendly on this point.

questions.question: The Fairchild exception is primarily confined to which specific type of case?

options.A: All toxic tort cases where the plaintiff was exposed to harmful substances by multiple defendants in succession
options.B: Mesothelioma cases where asbestos — a single disease-causing agent — can only cause the disease in one way, and multiple employers each negligently exposed the plaintiff to that agent
options.C: All industrial disease cases involving inhalation of any type of dust or harmful particulate matter in the workplace
options.D: Cases where the plaintiff can identify at least one specific employer whose exposure probably caused the disease

questions.explanation: The Fairchild exception [2002] UKHL 22 is currently primarily confined to mesothelioma. The conditions for the exception are specific: (1) a single causative agent (asbestos) that causes a single disease (mesothelioma) in a single way; (2) multiple defendants who each negligently exposed the plaintiff to that agent; and (3) scientific impossibility of identifying which defendant's specific exposure caused the disease. Other toxic tort cases generally cannot invoke Fairchild.

questions.question: In the context of novus actus interveniens, when does the plaintiff's own act fail to break the chain of causation?

options.A: Where the plaintiff's act was objectively unreasonable in the circumstances
options.B: Where the plaintiff was aware of the risk but nonetheless voluntarily chose to take it
options.C: Where the plaintiff's capacity for free and rational choice had been destroyed or significantly impaired by the consequences of the defendant's negligence
options.D: Where the plaintiff suffered from a pre-existing psychiatric disorder at the time of the original tortious act

questions.explanation: As illustrated by Corr v IBC Vehicles [2008], the plaintiff's own act fails to constitute a novus actus where their capacity for free and rational decision-making has been destroyed or significantly impaired by the foreseeable consequences of the defendant's negligence. Where the plaintiff could not truly exercise free choice — because the very capacity for such choice had been removed by the defendant's tort — the act cannot be treated as an independent, voluntary intervention.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarises the Bonnington-Wilsher distinction?

options.A: Bonnington applies to employment cases where the harmful agent is a solid; Wilsher applies to medical negligence cases involving liquids or gases
options.B: Bonnington applies where the plaintiff can identify the primary causative source; Wilsher applies where the cause is scientifically uncertain
options.C: Bonnington applies where the harm is irreversible; Wilsher applies where remedial treatment was available to the plaintiff
options.D: Bonnington applies where one harmful agent came from multiple sources — the material contribution doctrine may operate; Wilsher applies where there are multiple entirely distinct potential causes — the but-for test must be satisfied

questions.explanation: The Bonnington-Wilsher distinction is one of the most-tested areas in FE-1. Where one type of harmful agent (e.g., silica dust in Bonnington, brick dust in McGhee) came from multiple sources — one negligent, one not — the material contribution doctrine can resolve the causation problem. But where there are multiple completely different potential causes, only one attributable to the defendant's negligence (as in Wilsher's five distinct causes), the material contribution doctrine does not apply and the plaintiff must prove causation under the but-for test.
